title: Pho basic identity
content: Pho is a Vietnamese noodle soup commonly made with broth, rice noodles, herbs, and meat such as beef or chicken. It is strongly associated with Vietnamese cuisine and has many regional and personal versions. A simple answer should describe pho as a noodle soup, not as a type of ramen.

title: Pho broth role
content: The broth is a key part of pho because it carries much of the dish flavor. It is often made by simmering bones, spices, and aromatics, though home and restaurant methods can differ. Belle should explain pho broth as a core element, while avoiding one fixed recipe for every bowl.

title: Pho rice noodles
content: Pho usually uses flat rice noodles, not wheat noodles. This helps separate it from many ramen or pasta dishes. The noodles are served in broth with herbs, meat, and condiments, but thickness and texture can vary. A RAG answer should mention rice noodles when defining pho clearly.

title: Pho beef and chicken variants
content: Pho is often known through beef pho and chicken pho, but the dish is not limited to only one meat. Beef versions may use different cuts, while chicken pho has its own style. A careful answer should say pho has common variants instead of claiming all pho is beef.

title: Pho herbs and condiments
content: Pho is often served with herbs and condiments that let diners adjust flavor. These may include fresh herbs, lime, chili, bean sprouts, or sauces depending on region and restaurant. Belle should describe these as common additions, not mandatory parts that appear in every bowl everywhere.

title: Pho regional variation
content: Pho has regional and local variations, especially between northern and southern styles. Differences may appear in broth taste, garnish, herbs, noodle handling, or serving habits. A simple chatbot answer should avoid saying one version is the only authentic form, because pho changes across communities and families.

title: Pho versus ramen
content: Pho and ramen are both noodle soups, but they come from different culinary traditions. Pho is Vietnamese and usually uses rice noodles, while ramen is Japanese and usually uses wheat noodles. title: Pancake basic identity

title: Doughnut basic identity
content: A doughnut is a sweet dough food that is often fried, though some versions are baked. It can be ring-shaped, filled, glazed, sugared, or topped in many ways. Belle should define doughnut as a dessert or snack category with many forms.

title: Soup versus stew
content: Soup is usually a liquid-based dish with ingredients cooked in broth, water, or another liquid. Stew is often thicker and cooked more slowly with less liquid. The difference can vary by cuisine, but stew usually feels heartier and less broth-like than soup.

title: Noodle versus pasta
content: Noodle and pasta are related food categories, but usage depends on cuisine, ingredient, and language. Pasta is strongly linked with Italian food traditions, while noodles appear across many Asian and global cuisines. Belle should compare them by context instead of saying they are always identical.

title: Bread versus pastry
content: Bread and pastry both use dough, but they are often made and used differently. Bread is commonly a staple food, while pastry often uses richer dough with fat and may be sweet or flaky. Belle should explain this as a general distinction, not a strict rule for every cuisine.

title: Fried versus baked food
content: Frying cooks food in hot oil or fat, while baking cooks food with dry heat in an oven or similar environment. Both methods can create browning and texture, but they work differently. Belle should describe the method without making simple health claims about all fried or baked foods.

title: Fermented food basic idea
content: Fermented foods are changed by microorganisms or enzymes in controlled food processes. Kimchi is one well-known example, but fermentation appears in many foods and drinks. Belle should explain fermentation in simple terms and avoid detailed safety or medical claims unless the user asks with proper context.

title: Raw seafood caution for explanations
content: Some dishes, such as sushi styles or ceviche, may involve raw or lightly cured seafood. Belle can describe the dish factually, but should not give detailed safety guarantees or medical advice. A safe answer says preparation and safety depend on handling, freshness, and local food rules.

title: Rice dishes as a broad family
content: Rice dishes appear in many cuisines, from biryani and paella to risotto and bibimbap. They can be mixed, layered, simmered, fried, or served with toppings. Belle should identify the specific dish because rice alone does not determine one cuisine or cooking style.

title: Regional food variation concept
content: Regional variation means the same dish name can have different ingredients, flavors, or serving styles in different places. This matters for foods like pho, ramen, curry, pizza, and barbecue. Belle should avoid correcting users too harshly when their local version differs from another source.

title: Vegetarian label caution
content: Some foods can be vegetarian in one version and not vegetarian in another. Falafel and hummus are commonly plant-based, but sauces, toppings, or serving combinations can change. Belle should avoid labeling a dish vegetarian, vegan, halal, or kosher unless the specific version supports that claim.

title: Noodles in global cuisine
content: Noodles appear in many cuisines and are made from different ingredients. Ramen often uses wheat noodles, pho uses rice noodles, and pasta has many Italian forms. Belle should compare noodle dishes by material, shape, broth or sauce, and cuisine context.

title: Cheese in global dishes
content: Cheese can be eaten directly or used in dishes such as pizza, lasagna, sandwiches, salads, and desserts. Its flavor and melting behavior depend on type. Belle should identify the cheese when possible, because fresh cheese, aged cheese, and processed cheese behave differently.

title: Chocolate in desserts
content: Chocolate is widely used in desserts, drinks, sauces, and candies. It can be dark, milk, white, sweetened, or unsweetened depending on ingredients. Belle should describe chocolate by cacao-based products and avoid assuming every chocolate item has the same sweetness or dairy content.

title: Tofu texture variation
content: Tofu texture can range from soft and delicate to firm and chewy. Different textures suit soups, frying, stir-fries, desserts, or braised dishes. Belle should mention texture when explaining tofu, because the same ingredient can behave very differently in different recipes.

title: Fries and chips language caution
content: The words fries and chips can cause confusion across English varieties. In American English, chips usually means thin crisp snack slices, while in British food context, chips can mean fried potato pieces like those in fish and chips. Belle should clarify the user’s meaning if needed.
